edges.rationale: The classical problem: open alternatives vs causal fixity of action.
Compatibilism answers the threat determinism poses to freedom.
Hobbes is the canonical early classical compatibilist (liberty as absence of impediment).
Hume's reconciling project on liberty and necessity (Enquiry VIII).
Free will is standardly the control condition on responsible agency.
Williams coined 'moral luck' in his 1976 paper (with Nagel's reply).
The moral luck literature targets Kant's thesis that moral worth is immune to luck.
The moral patient vs moral agent distinction.
Moral status is a central organising concept of applied ethics (animals, embryos).
The trolley case is a constructed moral dilemma.
Foot introduced the runaway tram case in 1967.
Foot's case was built to probe the doctrine of double effect.
Locus classicus: Summa Theologiae II-II q.64 a.7 on self-defence.
Double effect is a principle of natural law casuistry.
Supererogatory acts are good beyond what duty requires.
Aristotle's account of deliberation and the practical syllogism.
Kant made pure practical reason the source of the moral law.
Eudaimonia is the ancient conception of human well-being or flourishing.
Justice is classically one of the cardinal virtues (Republic IV, NE V).
Rights function as deontological constraints on action.
Singer was central to establishing modern applied ethics (Practical Ethics).
Psychological egoism denies that genuinely altruistic motivation exists.
Socrates denied akrasia is possible: no one errs willingly (Protagoras 358d).
The standard branch distinction: the status of morality vs its first-order content.
Scanlon's 'Contractualism and Utilitarianism' (1982) frames them as rivals.
Natural law ethics is the moral theory of the scholastic tradition.
Classical utilitarianism's theory of the good is hedonistic (Bentham, Mill).
Hare's universal prescriptivism makes universalizability a condition on moral judgments.
Realism is a position within metaethics about the status of moral facts.
Anti-realism is the metaethical denial of stance-independent moral facts.
The definitional opposition over whether moral facts exist mind-independently.
Cognitivism is a metaethical thesis about moral judgment and truth-aptness.
Whether moral judgments express truth-apt beliefs or non-cognitive attitudes.
Non-cognitivism is the classic expression-based form of anti-realism.
Emotivism treats moral utterances as expressions of feeling, not beliefs.
Prescriptivism treats moral judgments as universal prescriptions, not descriptions.
Expressivism is the contemporary refinement of non-cognitivism.
Language, Truth and Logic ch. 6 gives the classic emotivist account.
Stevenson's Ethics and Language (1944) is the developed emotivism.
Hare's universal prescriptivism (The Language of Morals, 1952).
Blackburn's projectivist expressivism is one of its two canonical statements.
Gibbard's norm-expressivism (Wise Choices, Apt Feelings) extends the program.
Quasi-realism is Blackburn's own coinage and program.
Quasi-realism builds on an expressivist base to earn realist-sounding talk.
It aims to recover realist discourse without realist metaphysics.
Mackie: moral judgments are beliefs, and all systematically false.
Chapter 1 ('The Subjectivity of Values') states the error theory.
Error theory is the cognitivist form of anti-realism.
Nihilism denies that anything is morally right or wrong.
Subjectivism makes moral truth depend on attitudes of subjects.
Metaethical relativism relativises moral truth to cultures or frameworks.
Naturalist realism: moral facts are natural facts.
Non-naturalist realism: moral facts are sui generis, not natural.
The intra-realist dispute over the nature of moral properties.
Principia Ethica: good is a simple, non-natural, indefinable property.
Classical intuitionism (Moore, Prichard, Ross) is non-naturalist realism.
Prichard's 1912 paper is the arch-statement of Oxford intuitionism.
Ross systematised intuitionism with the prima facie duty framework.
Moore's charge that defining good in natural terms is fallacious.
The fallacy charge is aimed at naturalist definitions of good.
Moore's argument that any naturalist definition leaves an open question.
Its target is the identification of goodness with any natural property.
Ross's framework of conditional duties in The Right and the Good (1930).
Hutcheson's moral sense theory founded 18th-century sentimentalism.
Hume grounded moral distinctions in sentiment, not reason.
The 18th-century debate: moral sense vs reason as the source of morals.
Hume's observation that ought cannot be derived from is ('Hume's Law').
Particularist holism developed through critical engagement with Ross's framework.
Duty-based theory is one of the three main families of normative ethics.
Outcome-based theory is one of the three main families of normative ethics.
Character-based theory is one of the three main families of normative ethics.
The standard opposition in normative ethics: right-making by duty vs by outcomes.
Virtue ethics defines itself against duty-based theories (Anscombe 1958 framing).
Virtue ethics rejects outcome-maximisation as the criterion of right action.
Utilitarianism is the welfare-maximising species of consequentialism.
Applies the utility criterion directly to individual acts.
Applies the utility criterion to rules rather than individual acts.
The classic intra-utilitarian dispute: acts vs rules as the locus of assessment.
Bentham gave the classical formulation (Introduction to the Principles, 1789).
Mill refined the doctrine with qualitative distinctions among pleasures.
Sidgwick's Methods of Ethics is the third classical statement of utilitarianism.
Singer carried utilitarianism into contemporary applied ethics.
Bentham's principle of utility: greatest happiness of the greatest number.
The principle is the criterion of right action in classical utilitarianism.
Kant's ethics is the paradigm deontological theory.
Ross's pluralism of prima facie duties is the classic non-Kantian deontology.
Deontology (Greek deon) takes duty as its central normative notion.
Kant's supreme principle of morality.
Kant's own distinction: unconditional vs desire-dependent commands of reason.
The contrast class Kant defines in the Groundwork for conditional imperatives.
Autonomy as the will's self-legislation of the moral law is Kant's doctrine.
Kant's pair: self-legislation vs determination of the will from outside.
Kant coined the contrast term for wills governed by external incentives.
Groundwork I: the only thing good without qualification is a good will.
The CI is a test applied to the agent's maxims (subjective principles of action).
Kant's ideal of a systematic union of rational agents under common laws.
The kingdom of ends is one of Kant's formulations of the CI.
The universal-law formulation tests whether a maxim can be willed universally.
The Nicomachean Ethics is the founding text of the virtue tradition.
Hursthouse's On Virtue Ethics is the leading contemporary neo-Aristotelian statement.
Foot's work on virtues and natural goodness revived Aristotelian ethics.
After Virtue re-founded virtue ethics on tradition and practices.
Modern Moral Philosophy (1958) sparked the 20th-century revival of virtue ethics.
Excellences of character are the theory's basic evaluative notion.
Aristotle made flourishing the final end of human life (NE I).
Mainstream virtue ethics is eudaimonist: virtues are constitutive of flourishing.
Aristotle's doctrine that virtue lies in a mean between excess and deficiency.
The doctrine of the mean is Aristotle's account of the structure of virtue.
Phronesis, the intellectual virtue governing right action (NE VI).
Practical wisdom unifies the character virtues in Aristotelian theory.
NE VII contains the canonical analysis of weakness of will.
Hobbesian mutual-advantage contract theory is the root of contractarianism.
Contractarian moral theory generalises the social contract tradition.
Scanlon's principles no one could reasonably reject define contractualism.
Standard distinction: Kantian reasonable rejection vs Hobbesian mutual advantage.
Leviathan gives the founding modern statement of the social contract.
Rawls revived the contract device at a higher level of abstraction (justice as fairness).
Standard classification: maximising the good for the agent alone.
Self-interest vs other-regard as the basis of right action.
Standard distinction: descriptive thesis about motives vs normative thesis about duty.
Epicurus is the canonical ancient proponent of pleasure as the good.
Voluntarist grounding of morality in God's will vs grounding in rational nature.
Aquinas's Summa account is the canonical natural law ethics.
In a Different Voice (1982) originated the ethics of care.
Noddings's Caring (1984) gave care ethics its systematic statement.
Care ethics is the best-known strand of feminist moral theory.
Hume interrupted Kant's dogmatic slumber (Prolegomena preface).
Kant was trained in the Leibniz-Wolffian rationalist school.
The movement (Fichte, Schelling, Hegel) grew out of the critical philosophy.
Schopenhauer built his system on Kant's transcendental idealism.
Schopenhauer as Educator; Nietzsche's early philosophy is Schopenhauerian.
Mill was raised within Bentham's circle as his intellectual heir.
Sidgwick's utilitarianism develops out of Mill's (Methods preface).
Singer is explicitly Sidgwickian (The Point of View of the Universe).
Parfit regarded Sidgwick's Methods as the best book on ethics.
Moore attended Sidgwick's Cambridge lectures; Principia engages him throughout.
Ross acknowledges Prichard's influence; both Oxford intuitionists.
Socrates is Plato's teacher and dialogical protagonist.
Twenty years in Plato's Academy.
Aquinas synthesised Aristotle ('the Philosopher') with Christian theology.
Hume's moral sentimentalism develops Hutcheson's moral sense theory.
Ayer presented logical positivism as the heir of Hume's empiricism.
Blackburn's projectivism is self-described neo-Humeanism.
Korsgaard is the leading contemporary Kantian constructivist.
Justice as fairness is presented as Kantian constructivism.
Scanlon's contractualism develops within the Rawlsian contract tradition.
After Virtue defends an Aristotelian tradition-based ethics.
Her virtue ethics is explicitly neo-Aristotelian.
Zeno founded the Stoa around 300 BCE.
Epicurus founded the Garden and its school.
The rival Hellenistic schools: virtue as sole good vs pleasure as the good.
The classic early modern opposition over the sources of knowledge.
High scholasticism built its method and ethics on recovered Aristotle.
Williams's integrity objection (Smart & Williams 1973).
Butler's Fifteen Sermons contain the classic refutation of psychological egoism.
Foot's 'Moral Beliefs' and related papers attack Hare's prescriptivism.
Kant, 1785.
Kant, 1788 (the second Critique).
Kant, 1781/1787 (the first Critique).
Kant, 1797 — distinct from the 1785 Groundwork.
Aristotle's principal ethical treatise.
Plato's dialogue on justice; not to be confused with Aristotle's Politics.
Hobbes, 1651.
Hume, 1751 (the second Enquiry).
Bentham, 1789.
Mill, 1861/1863 — Mill, not Bentham, wrote the book titled Utilitarianism.
Mill, 1859.
Sidgwick, 1874.
Moore, 1903.
Ayer, 1936.
Mackie, 1977.
Ross, 1930.
Rawls, 1971.
MacIntyre, 1981.
Parfit, 1984.
Scanlon, 1998.
Nietzsche, 1887 — not Schopenhauer.
Anscombe's 1958 paper in Philosophy.
First formulated in the Groundwork (1785), not in either Critique.
Groundwork I opens with the good will claim.
The kingdom of ends formulation appears in Groundwork II.
Bentham states the principle of utility in ch. 1 (1789).
The doctrine of the mean is set out in NE II.
Principia Ethica ch. 1 (1903).
The term is Moore's, coined in Principia Ethica — not in Sidgwick's Methods.
The is/ought passage is Treatise III.i.1 — not in the second Enquiry.
Ross introduces prima facie duties in ch. 2 (1930).
Anscombe coined the term 'consequentialism' in this 1958 paper.
After Virtue's opening chapters attack emotivism as the culture's de facto ethics.
Anscombe's paper attacks consequentialism (her coinage, used pejoratively).
Rawls frames justice as fairness explicitly against utilitarianism (TJ sec. 5-6).
